text: The robotic posterior exenteration for locally advanced rectal cancer is demonstrated using the Da Vinci Xi platform. Four robotic ports are inserted in a straight line above the right anterior superior iliac spine and umbilicus. The dissection begins with creating a plane under the inferior mesenteric vein, followed by medial to lateral mobilization of the rectal tube. The inferior mesenteric artery is dissected and divided beyond the left colic artery origin. The descending colon and splenic flexure are mobilized, and posterior rectal mobilization continues to the pelvic floor. Lateral dissection involves tackling the infundibulopelvic ligaments, with the ureter identified and the infundibulopelvic ligament lifted off retroperitoneal tissue. The posterior leaf of the broad ligament is divided to isolate and clip the infundibulopelvic ligament.

The surgeon begins lateral dissection by addressing the infundibulopelvic ligaments. The peritoneum is divided along the line of TALT, and the ureter is identified. The infundibulopelvic ligament is lifted off the retroperitoneal tissue, and the posterior leaf of the broad ligament is divided to isolate the infundibulopelvic ligament, which is then clipped and divided.

The surgeon fully opens the posterior leaf of the broad ligament and mobilizes the parametral tissue medially. The round ligament is cauterized and divided. The ureter is mobilized medially to facilitate dissection of the uterine vessels at their root. The obliterated umbilical artery is traced to its origin by dividing the overlying soft tissue, revealing the origin of the uterine vessels through gentle dissection.

Following the division of the infundibulopelvic ligament, the surgeon fully opens the posterior leaf of the broad ligament and mobilizes the parametral tissue medially to ensure clear margins. The round ligament is cauterized and divided. The ureter is mobilized medially, aiding in the dissection of the uterine vessels at their root. The obliterated umbilical artery is traced down to its origin by dividing the overlying soft tissue, and gentle dissection distal to this point reveals the origin of the uterine vessels.

The surgeon dissects, clips, and divides the uterine artery and vein individually on both sides. The anterior peritoneum is then divided to mobilize the bladder away from the cervix and upper part of the vagina. The anterior leaf of the broad ligament appears very short due to the patient's postmenopausal status.

The surgeon dissects, clips, and divides the uterine artery and vein individually on both sides. The anterior peritoneum is then divided to mobilize the bladder away from the cervix and upper part of the vagina. The anterior leaf of the broad ligament appears very short due to the patient's postmenopausal status.

The surgeon performs an anterior colpotomy after mobilizing the anterior vaginal wall. The incision is extended laterally, and the soft tissue beyond the vaginal walls is cauterized to address the descending uterine vessels and vaginal arteries.

The surgeon performs an anterior colpotomy after mobilizing the anterior vaginal wall, extending the incision laterally. Before completing the lateral cuts, the soft tissue beyond the vaginal walls is cauterized to manage the descending uterine vessels and vaginal arteries.

The surgeon divides the posterior vaginal wall and dissects it away from the anterior wall of the rectum, ensuring safe closure of the vaginal vault and adequate mobilization of the distal rectal stump for colorectal anastomosis. The distal rectal transection is performed intracorporeally using an endostapler, requiring two firings to complete. The specimen, including the rectosigmoid, uterus, vagina, and tubes, is delivered outside through the future ileostomy site, with the proximal bowel division performed extracorporeally.

The surgeon divides the posterior vaginal wall and dissects it away from the anterior wall of the rectum, ensuring safe closure of the vaginal vault and adequate mobilization of the distal rectal stump for colorectal anastomosis. The distal rectal transection is performed intracorporeally using an endostapler, requiring two firings to complete. The specimen, including the rectosigmoid, uterus, vagina, and tubes, is delivered outside through the future ileostomy site. The proximal bowel division is done extracorporeally, and the vaginal vault is closed with continuous sutures.

The surgeon closes the vaginal vault with continuous sutures using a synthetic absorbable suture material. Following this, a colorectal anastomosis is performed with a circular stapler, and a diversion ileostomy is created.
The surgeon performs a colorectal anastomosis using a circular stapler, followed by the creation of a diversion ileostomy.
